title: A Computational Model of Linguistic Humor in Puns
research_questions: Can information-theoretic measures derived from a probabilistic model of sentence processing predict human judgments of humor in puns?
Do ambiguity and distinctiveness distinguish phonetic puns from non-pun sentences that also contain phonetically ambiguous words?
Within a set of puns, can the proposed measures predict fine-grained differences in funniness?
tasks: identifying semantic support for alternative pun interpretations

datasets_used.name: Phonetic pun and non-pun sentence set
datasets_used.size: 435 sentences: 65 identical-homophone puns, 130 corresponding non-pun sentences, 80 near-homophone puns, and 160 corresponding non-pun sentences

datasets_used.name: Pun of the Day
datasets_used.size: over a thousand puns submitted by users; 40 identical-homophone puns and 80 near-homophone puns collected from a pun website

datasets_used.name: Heinle’s Newbury House Dictionary of American English
datasets_used.size: 130 non-pun sentences for identical homophones and 160 corresponding non-pun sentences for near homophones

datasets_used.name: Funniness ratings for phonetic pun and non-pun sentence set
datasets_used.size: 100 participants rated the 195 identical homophone sentences; 160 participants rated the 240 near homophone sentences; seven and four non-native-English participants were removed

datasets_used.name: Semantic relatedness word-pair ratings
datasets_used.size: 1,460 distinct word pairs for identical homophone sentences and 2,056 word pairs for near homophone sentences; 200 and 120 participants rated them, with five and two non-native-English participants removed

methods_proposed.name: Noisy-channel gist-level sentence-processing model for puns
methods_proposed.summary: The paper models a phonetically ambiguous sentence as evidence for a latent sentence meaning, approximated by the observed ambiguous word and its homophone or near homophone. Each content word is generated either from semantic relatedness to the latent meaning or from a noise process based on n-gram statistics.

methods_proposed.name: Ambiguity
methods_proposed.summary: Ambiguity is defined as the entropy of the posterior distribution over the two candidate sentence meanings, P(m|w). High entropy indicates that both meanings are similarly likely in context.

methods_proposed.name: Distinctiveness
methods_proposed.summary: Distinctiveness is defined as the symmetrized Kullback-Leibler divergence between the distributions over semantically relevant words given each candidate meaning. High distinctiveness indicates that the two meanings are supported by different subsets of words in the sentence.

humor_theories.detail: Ambiguity operationalizes the presence of incongruous sentence meanings, while distinctiveness is described as roughly corresponding to resolution through different supporting contexts.
The discussion relates the model to Raskin’s claim that a funny text activates two incompatible scripts.
The discussion compares the work with Attardo and Raskin’s General Theory of Verbal Humor and later ontological-semantics approaches.

evaluation_methods.detail: Amazon Mechanical Turk participants rated sentence funniness on a 1 to 7 scale; ratings were z-scored within participant and averaged across participants.
Amazon Mechanical Turk participants rated semantic relatedness for word pairs on a 1 to 10 scale; these ratings were used to estimate word-meaning relatedness in the model.
The paper evaluates ambiguity and distinctiveness using t-tests, linear mixed-effects regression, F-test/R^2, Pearson correlations, and quartile comparisons against human funniness ratings.

headline_results.finding: Ambiguity was significantly higher for pun sentences than non-pun sentences.
Distinctiveness was significantly higher for pun sentences than non-pun sentences.
A model using ambiguity and distinctiveness explained a substantial amount of reliable variance in averaged funniness ratings.
In the linear mixed-effects regression, both measures were positive significant predictors of funniness.
headline_results.value: Ambiguity estimate 1.915, SE 0.221, p < .0001; Distinctiveness estimate 0.264, SE 0.040, p < .0001

headline_results.finding: Within puns, distinctiveness predicted fine-grained funniness, whereas ambiguity did not.
headline_results.value: distinctiveness r = .28, p < .001; ambiguity r = .03, p = .697

headline_results.finding: Puns in the top distinctiveness quartile were significantly funnier than puns in lower quartiles.

key_findings: Phonetic puns cluster higher than non-pun sentences on both ambiguity and distinctiveness, even though the non-puns also contain phonetically ambiguous words.
Ambiguity helps distinguish puns from non-puns, but it does not explain which puns are funnier within the pun set.
Distinctiveness significantly correlates with human funniness ratings within puns and identifies especially funny puns in the top quartile.

The model can also identify words most likely to support each interpretation of a pun, producing qualitatively plausible explanations such as linking “magician” and “hare” versus “mad,” “pulled,” and “hair.”
Identical-homophone and near-homophone puns did not differ significantly in funniness ratings, ambiguity scores, or distinctiveness scores.
limitations: The study is limited to written sentences containing phonetic ambiguity, especially homophone and near-homophone puns.
Sentence meaning is approximated coarsely by the meaning of the ambiguous word and its homophone, capturing only the gist rather than full compositional sentence meaning.
The model relies on human semantic relatedness judgments to estimate P(w_i|m), because automatic corpus-based estimates were difficult for sparse commonsense word pairs.
The relatedness of a word with itself is treated as a free parameter and fitted to people’s funniness judgments, resulting in r = 13.
The paper notes that more complex jokes may require models of time-course sentence processing, pragmatic reasoning, background knowledge, and multisentence discourse.
The paper does not examine spoken sentences, where ambiguity cannot be partially resolved by orthographic form.

human_samples.population: Pun of the Day website users
human_samples.observations: At collection time the website contained over a thousand user-submitted puns; the authors collected 40 identical-homophone puns and 80 near-homophone puns from it.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number or demographics of website contributors.

human_samples.observations: Generated 25 additional identical-homophone pun sentences based on a separate list of homophone words.
human_samples.detail: Used because only a limited number of website puns satisfied the identical-homophone criterion.

human_samples.participant_count: 100 recruited; 7 removed for reporting a native language other than English; 93 retained
human_samples.population: Amazon Mechanical Turk participants; retained participants reported English as native language
human_samples.observations: Funniness ratings for the 195 sentences containing identical homophones. Each participant read roughly 60 sentences in random order, counterbalanced for sentence types, and rated each sentence from 1 (not at all funny) to 7 (extremely funny).
human_samples.detail: Ratings were z-scored within participant and averaged across participants. The paper states sample sizes were chosen so each sentence would receive roughly 20–30 funniness ratings.

human_samples.participant_count: 160 recruited; 4 removed for reporting a native language other than English; 156 retained
human_samples.population: Amazon Mechanical Turk participants; retained participants reported English as native language
human_samples.observations: Funniness ratings for the 240 near-homophone sentences. Each participant read 40 sentences in random order, counterbalanced for sentence types, and rated each sentence from 1 to 7.
human_samples.detail: Ratings were z-scored within participant and averaged across participants.

human_samples.participant_count: 200 recruited; 5 removed for reporting a native language other than English; 195 retained
human_samples.population: Amazon Mechanical Turk participants; retained participants reported English as native language
human_samples.observations: Semantic relatedness ratings for 1,460 distinct word pairs from identical-homophone sentences. Each participant saw 146 word pairs in random order and rated relatedness on a 1–10 scale.
human_samples.detail: Average z-scored relatedness ratings were used to estimate R(w_i, m), which the model treats as an approximation to PMI.

human_samples.participant_count: 120 recruited; 2 removed for reporting a native language other than English; 118 retained
human_samples.population: Amazon Mechanical Turk participants; retained participants reported English as native language
human_samples.observations: Semantic relatedness ratings for 2,056 distinct word pairs from near-homophone sentences, on a 1–10 scale.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report how many near-homophone word pairs each participant rated.

judges.name: Amazon Mechanical Turk funniness raters
judges.detail: Provided the human funniness judgments used as the dependent variable for all 435 sentences; ratings were z-scored within participant and averaged.

judges.name: Amazon Mechanical Turk semantic relatedness raters
judges.detail: Provided word-pair relatedness ratings used to estimate P(w_i|m) in the computational model.

judges.name: Authors and research assistant
judges.detail: Selected puns from Pun of the Day, selected corresponding non-pun dictionary sentences, identified homophone or near-homophone alternatives, and added 25 research-assistant-generated identical-homophone puns. No independent validation of pun/non-pun labels is reported.

judges.name: Ambiguity and distinctiveness measures
judges.detail: Ambiguity was computed as entropy of P(m|w); distinctiveness was computed as symmetrized KL divergence between distributions over semantically relevant words. These measures determined the model scores used in the headline analyses.

judges.name: Statistical analyses
judges.detail: The reported results use t-tests, a linear mixed-effects regression, an F-test/R^2 analysis on averaged ratings, Pearson correlations, and a quartile comparison.

generation_selection.detail: The study scores human-collected or human-authored sentence stimuli. A research assistant generated 25 pun stimuli, but there is no model output sampling and no best-of-N selection.

model_provenance.name: Noisy-channel gist-level sentence-processing model for phonetic puns
model_provenance.detail: Custom model proposed in the paper. It computes ambiguity and distinctiveness for each sentence using two candidate meanings: the observed phonetically ambiguous word and its homophone or near-homophone. No software version, code release, or run date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Trigram language-model/noise component based on Google N-grams
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: Google Web 1T 5-gram Version 1 / LDC2006T13 is cited for Brants & Franz (2006)
model_provenance.detail: Used to compute P(w_i|bigram_i) for words treated as noise. The paper does not report smoothing, preprocessing, or zero-count handling.

model_provenance.name: Google Web unigrams frequency estimates
model_provenance.detail: Used to obtain P(w_i) in the transformation from relatedness ratings to P(w_i|m). The exact snapshot/version is not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Scoring the 435 sentence stimuli with the custom ambiguity/distinctiveness model
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 2 candidate sentence meanings per sentence: the observed ambiguous word h and its homophone or near-homophone h'
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: No random sampling reported. The model uses deterministic formulas: entropy for ambiguity and symmetrized KL divergence for distinctiveness. It assumes equal prior probability over subsets of semantically relevant words; P(m) is approximated using unigram frequency; the self-relatedness parameter r was fit to funniness judgments, resulting in r = 13.
inference_budgets.detail: One ambiguity score and one distinctiveness score were computed for each of 435 sentences. The model also computed argmax_f P(f|m,w) for qualitative examples of semantically relevant words. Runtime, hardware, implementation, and monetary cost are not reported.
The paper reports using Google N-grams for P(w_i|bigram_i) and Google Web unigrams for P(w_i), but does not report lookup counts, smoothing choices, preprocessing, or computational cost.

human_baselines.description: Human funniness ratings were the criterion variable and human semantic relatedness ratings were model inputs; the paper does not report a separate human baseline predictor or human-vs-model performance comparison.
human_baselines.detail: There are no model-generated outputs evaluated alongside human-authored outputs, and no matched human baseline for predicting the sentence-level funniness scores.

mitigations: The 435-sentence evaluation set and the funniness/semantic-relatedness ratings were collected or constructed for this study rather than taken from a preexisting rated benchmark.
No pretrained LLM or proprietary generative model is evaluated.
contamination_risk.basis: This is a custom deterministic computational model using newly collected human ratings, not a later pretrained model evaluated on a public legacy benchmark. Some source material came from public websites/dictionaries, and the self-relatedness parameter r was fit on the same funniness judgments used in evaluation, which is a validation/overfitting concern rather than classic benchmark-contamination. The paper does not discuss contamination or decontamination.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Primary empirical claims are based on Mechanical Turk funniness judgments, Mechanical Turk semantic relatedness ratings, automatic model-derived measures, and statistical tests; no LLM judge is used.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: No LLMs are reported as evaluators, annotators, metrics, or model-output selectors.

reporting_gaps: No participant demographics beyond exclusion by self-reported non-English native language; age, gender, geography, education, English proficiency criteria, and MTurk qualifications are not reported.
MTurk compensation, recruitment dates, consent/IRB details, and exact participant assignment procedures are not reported.
Blindness of participants to study hypotheses and to pun/non-pun categories is not reported.
Exact number of funniness ratings per sentence after exclusions is not reported; the paper only states roughly 20–30 ratings per sentence.
For the near-homophone semantic relatedness task, the number of word pairs seen by each participant and assignment/counterbalancing details are not reported.

Stimulus-selection procedures are only partially specified: the criteria for choosing puns from Pun of the Day, the separate homophone list used by the research assistant, and dictionary-sentence selection details are not fully reported.
No independent validation of pun/non-pun labels is reported.
The function-word removal list, preprocessing details, code implementation, and full handling of homophones/near-homophones are not reported in the paper.
Google Web unigram version/snapshot is not reported; n-gram smoothing, normalization, and zero-count handling are not reported.
Model runtime, hardware, compute budget, and monetary cost are not reported.
The free self-relatedness parameter r was fit to people’s funniness judgments and evaluated on the same sentence set; no held-out test split or cross-validation is reported.
No baseline computational models or human predictor baseline are reported for comparison with ambiguity and distinctiveness.
